author: @kyleolson8977
text:
It's worth nothing that the two films that moved Seagal up a level, first Above the Law and then Under Siege, were both directed by "The Fugitive" director Andrew Davis. If you watch Under Siege, you can see that the good actors (Tommy Lee Jones, Gary Busey) and the entire movie are acting around Seagal.
It's possible, just possible, that it was the director and everyone else on set who was what made these movies work.

replies.text:
I mean, if I'm being honest with myself, Seagal wasn't bad in Under Siege. Sure, you can already smell his ego with how he goes through the whole movie without suffering a scratch until he got anchored, but the movie as a whole was still a guilty pleasure for me to rewatch time and time again. Did Tommy, Gary and a certain Cake scene elevate the movie? Most certainly. But it just feels like a fun time in general for me. 



Then again, Under Siege 1 and 2 are the ONLY Steven Seagal movies I've ever seen. Never really cared for the guy or his other movies.

replies.author: @mikepatton8691
replies.text: ​@faynarawn4164there's a good reason you were surprised he agreed to do that, he didn't. Apparently it was John Leguizamo who was supposed to die early and Seagal was originally meant to survive and play the role Leguizamo did after they boarded the plane. However Kurt Russell couldn't stand Seagal and Seagal was being his usual self, difficult, none of the cast or crew could stand being around him. So the director changed things and killed off Seagal early to get him off set as quickly as possible. His head was originally supposed to explode from depressurization but Seagal locked himself in his trailer, refusing to film the scene.

I already posted this elsewhere in these comments, but it directly applies here more than anywhere else, so, here goes again.   ;)

Aside from the factual BS that filled every moment that I have been able to stomach of the movie "on Deadly Ground" (Riding horses from the North Slope to Prince Williams sound??? Horses??? Traveling any distance at all in Alaska???  and that is not the worse of it...). Aside from that typical Steven Seagal stupidity, the only thing we remember this movie for up here is Stevie boy demanding that the coordinator for the dog team scenes used in the movie (Libby Riddles, the first woman to win the Iditarod and a bit of a local hero/legend) take a team into Valdez from their location camp  up on a mountain glacier in the area, and bring him back a pair of hookers. Aside from being such an absurd demand for so many different reasons,, it could not have been more insulting. It is probably a very very good thing that he was already out of Alaska before the story became commonly known. Speaking from a purely personal position... and hypothetically of course... I would have taken great joy in a bit of a sparring round with this "great one" and seeing just how far the red would spread through the snow as he lay unconscious covered in his own piss and snot. Or just sitting back and watching all the other men (and a few women I expect) waiting in line for their shot at him. Even beyond his total lack of acting ability or grip on reality, this loser is a complete dirtbag and a waste of human flesh. I wouldn't even feed him to the dogs. Doubtful that they would touch him anyway. They'd probably take one sniff and go looking for a gut pile somewhere instead.

author: @screenwolf
text:
You forgot one thing: The absolute best film of Seagal's career - Executive Decision. Which was a genius move that cashed in perfectly on the timing of audiences reaching peak Segal nonsense fatigue by playing that one absolutely perfect cinematic bait and switch. Starting the movie with Segal playing a standard Seagal character to the audible eye roll of everyone in the cinema, only for moments late to unceremoniously kill off his character and hand the reigns of the movie to the infinitely more liked and talented Kurt Russel and to excited cheers from the that same audience.  It was frankly brilliant. 

And that is how I will always remember Steven Seagal. The one action star the entire audience disliked so much you could actually pull a bait and switch and the audience will love you for it!

replies.text: @rimjobledouche5201 Well, I think they exist everywhere. But in the USSR, there was the Iron Curtain, and anything Western attracted people, especially towards the end of the USSR. Especially cinema. We didn't have much to compare it to:  who could tell a good film from a bad one if we didn't know it was bad? There were no film critics back then. Then the 1990s began in Russia,with all the troubles of that time, people needed simple things to take their minds off things, so films with Seagal or Van Damme were warmly received. Good guy with gun and fist bring justice Happy end. There were even fans of Dudikoff's films, because NINJA! :)

author: @jp3813
text:
80s & 90s Hong Kong martial arts cinema had the likes of Jackie Chan, Jet Li, Donnie Yen, Sammo Hung, Yuen Biao, & Michelle Yeoh as its biggest stars. 
Hollywood: "Eh, screw dubbing & subtitles, let's get that Steven Seagal guy."

replies.author: @jp3813
replies.text: @h4ven88 JC attempted to break into Hollywood in the 80s but the studios kept trying to change his style. In the mid-90s, Rumble in the Bronx became a hit in America which resulted in more of his Hong Kong movies being released there and US studios began casting more HK action stars: Chow Yun-fat (The Replacement Killers), Michelle Yeoh (Tomorrow Never Dies), Jet Li (Lethal Weapon 4), Jackie Chan (Rush Hour), Sammo Hung (the TV show Martial Law), etc...

replies.author: @Tc4ify
replies.text: You are probably closer to the truth than you even intended. At the time, East Asian martial arts were all the rage, but most of the really good guys had pretty poor English at best. Enter Seagal who looks like he could be at least part Asian (well, at least at the time), did enough "moves" to convince the executives and voila...

replies.author: @jp3813
replies.text: @Tc4ify It does seem like the late 90s was when American audiences became very interested in entertainment outside of the US: action flicks from Hong Kong, anime & super sentai from Japan, Latino music, etc... By the time we got to the 2000s, many westerners didn't shy away from subtitles anymore: Crouching Tiger Hidden Dragon, Hero, Fearless, Amelie, Pan's Labyrinth, The Passion of the Christ, Apocalypto, Babel, Inglourious Basterds, etc...

author: @hellsunicorn
text:
Steven Seagal is living proof that even being the luckiest guy on earth isn’t foolproof. He had just enough skill to appear competent on camera and was blessed with brilliant costars who could fill the dynamic gaps between his wooden, one-dimensional performance. The problems were his ego and his mouth, the latter burned every bridge others built for him, the former alienated his fan base. He was genuinely a hot item for about 5 years, and while everything he did before Under Siege was basically the same story with different bad guys, it worked and resulted in some movies that are still quite watchable. He was never in the same league as Chuck Norris or Van Damme, but he was good at what he did. Him thinking he could be a director and putting out a piece of crap like On Deadly Ground killed his momentum, and while some of the stuff he did afterward wasn’t bad (The Glimmer Man was a solid Keenan Ivory Wayans flick with Seagal being a passable Kato-style sidekick), he never recovered.

As a person, Seagal is a total dirtbag, but as Voltaire said, ‘It is sometimes necessary to separate the man from the work’.
